items.title: 23-year-old snowmobiler dies in Kootenay Pass avalanche; forecasters warn of high risks in B.C.’s backcountry
items.summary_auto: A snowmobiler has died in B.C.’s Interior after an avalanche in the Kootenay Pass area on Saturday, as forecasters warn hazardous conditions persist across the province.

items.title: The must-see red carpet looks from the 2026 Actor Awards
items.summary_auto: Wunmi Mosaku, Teyana Taylor, Kate Hudson, Mia Goth and more standout looks from the SAG-AFTRA celebration.

items.title: Celebrations in downtown Halifax mark fall of Iran's supreme leader
items.summary_auto: More than a hundred Iranian-Canadians took to the streets of Halifax on Sunday to celebrate after U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran that killed the country's supreme leader.

items.title: Toronto student jailed in Pakistan over social media content denied bail
items.summary_auto: The Canadian University of Toronto PhD student who was arrested while conducting research for his dissertation in Pakistan has been denied bail.

items.title: Montreal Victoire blank Minnesota Frost 4-0 to take 1st place in PWHL standings
items.summary_auto: Ann-Renée Desbiens earned her fourth shutout of the season with a 17-save effort as the Montreal Victoire defeated the Minnesota Frost 4-0 in Professional Women's Hockey League action at Place Bell on Sunday.

items.title: Oil jumps 10% on Iran conflict and could spike to $100 US a barrel, analysts say
items.summary_auto: Brent crude jumped 10 per cent to about $80 US a barrel over the counter on Sunday, oil traders said, while analysts predicted that prices could climb  as high as $100 US after U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran plunged the Middle East into ​a new war.

items.title: Hundreds of thousands stranded as war shuts down much of Middle East air traffic
items.summary_auto: Hundreds of thousands of stranded travellers scrambled to make new connections and get through to airlines on jammed phone lines on Sunday after the attack on Iran by the United States and Israel shut down much of the Middle East to air travel.

items.title: Conservatives call for federal independent inquiry on Tumbler Ridge mass shooting
items.summary_auto: The federal Conservatives are calling for an independent inquiry on the mass shooting in Tumbler Ridge, B.C., to understand what happened and how to prevent similar tragedies from occurring in the future.

items.title: President of Iran's soccer federation says World Cup participation in U.S. is in doubt
items.summary_auto: The president of Iran's soccer federation says he does not know if the national team can play 2026 FIFA World Cup matches in the United States following the surprise U.S. and Israeli bombardment of his country.

items.title: Iranian in N.L. grapples with mixed feelings as the U.S. and Israel launch strikes against Iran
items.summary_auto: Shayan Morshedi stayed awake, waiting for news from home, as the United States and Israel launched attacks on Iran on Saturday. Originally from Iran, the Memorial University PhD student said he messaged his parents and friends as soon as the first strikes hit the country Saturday morning.

items.title: U.S. hockey star Hilary Knight takes shot at Trump on SNL episode with Connor Storrie and Olympic champs
items.summary_auto: This time it was Hilary Knight's turn to provide a big laugh, with an assist from the Saturday Night Live writers, in the wake of a weeklong dust-up involving the United States women's and men's Olympic gold medal-winning hockey teams.

items.title: U.S. hockey star Hilary Knight delivers joke on SNL featuring women's, men's Olympic champions
items.summary_auto: This time it was Hilary Knight's turn to provide a big laugh, with an assist from the Saturday Night Live writers, in the wake of a weeklong dust-up involving the United States women's and men's Olympic gold medal-winning hockey teams.

items.title: Canada's Katelyn Fung, Kate Miller capture 1st international diving medal together
items.summary_auto: Katelyn Fung and Kate Miller claimed their first international podium finish as a team in the synchronized 10-metre event at the Montreal Diving World Cup on Saturday.

items.title: Say it ain't sew: B.C. to end PST exemption for yarn, other materials used to make or mend clothing
items.summary_auto: Yarn and other materials used to make or mend clothing will no longer be exempt from provincial sales tax as of Oct. 1, a move that the owner of a Kelowna, B.C., yarn store says unfairly affects people who are looking for a more mindful and sustainable approach to fashion.

items.title: Why killing Ayatollah Ali Khamenei may not mean regime change in Iran
items.summary_auto: Following a joint U.S.-Israeli missile strike that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran is experiencing a volatile mix of state-sanctioned mourning and celebratory street protests. The assassination has sparked international fear that the lack of a post-war plan could plunge the region into uncontrollable chaos.

items.title: Why were toxic drug deaths in B.C. down 21% in 2025?
items.summary_auto: A new study has found that decreasing fentanyl concentrations in the illicit drug supply was related with a decrease in fatal overdoses in B.C. But scientists, advocates and officials remain unclear on why exactly the drug death toll in the province dropped by 21 per cent in 2025 compared to the previous year.

items.title: Hailing India as a 'natural partner,' Carney says Canada can secure a free trade deal by year's end
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney's Indian charm offensive continued over the weekend as he set aside any notion of bilateral bad blood and hailed the South Asian country as a "natural partner."

items.title: N.S. premier booed at African Heritage Month gala as budget cuts spark upset
items.summary_auto: Tim Houston was booed as he delivered remarks at the African Heritage Month gala in Halifax on Saturday night. Houston's government delivered a budget last week that includes cuts to programs aimed at Black and African Nova Scotian communities.

items.title: Alberta’s reliance on oil revenues means that when prices fall, the economy follows
items.summary_auto: The Alberta government's new budget projects years of deficits due to slumping oil prices. Some experts say the province’s predictions for price recovery may be optimistic.

items.title: Forget white sand beaches. These snowbirds prefer to prowl for owls on the frozen Canadian prairie
items.summary_auto: Southern Manitoba is a winter hot spot for tourists who prefer seeking out elusive owls on the frozen prairie over beachside getaways.

items.title: Family believes Nova Scotia's new medical records system played part in baby's death
items.summary_auto: A Musquodoboit, N.S., family says it believes a tragedy could have been averted if not for the One Person One Record system that launched in December at the IWK Health Centre.

items.title: Nova Scotia's craft beer industry seeing 'unheard of' number of closures
items.summary_auto: While Nova Scotia's craft beer industry saw huge growth in the past couple of decades, growing to as many as 60 breweries with some having additional taproom locations, the number of breweries has decreased to around 50 in just the last two years.

items.title: The rise of singlehood could impact everything from housing to taxes
items.summary_auto: For decades there has been an increase in single people across the world. But more recently, that number seems to be not only growing but changing how we talk about single people and how the world might work with more of them.

items.title: Canada poised to become 'one of the largest suppliers of LNG in the world': energy minister
items.summary_auto: Canada's energy minister says the country is poised to become one of the world's biggest suppliers of liquefied natural gas, exporting as much as 100 million tonnes per year.

items.title: Canada should not have an election before reviewing CUSMA trade deal, says Poilievre
items.summary_auto: Conservative Leader Pierre Polievre says Canada should not have an election before it reviews the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) this year — and instead he's reiterating his proposal the Carney government set up an all-party committee on the matter.

items.title: Why much of Canada's national art collection isn't online
items.summary_auto: Canada has the technology to share its national art collection online. What it hasn’t clearly decided is how much copyright law should stand in the way.

items.title: Snowbanks are still blocking parking on some streets more than a month after Toronto's last big storm
items.summary_auto: More than a month after Toronto's last major snowstorm, some residents are complaining their streets are still so clogged with curbside snowbanks that finding on-street parking remains a problem — not just for residents but local businesses too.
